1: A type: "Out of the depths I cry to you, O LORD!" The cry from the maʿamāqqîm (depths, deep waters) is the cry of Israel in extremity — and the cry of Christ from the cross. Matthew 27:46 = Psalms 22:1 — "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" Christ entered the deepest depths — Ephesians 4:9 ("he also descended into the lower, earthly regions") — so that no depth of human suffering falls outside his solidarity and redemptive reach.
2: A type: "O Lord, hear my voice! Let your ears be attentive to the cry of my pleas for mercy!" The intercessory cry that Scripture records as answered — God heard. This is the pattern of Christ's own prayer in Gethsemane and at the cross: Hebrews 5:7 — "he offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence." The Father heard and raised him.
3: A direct: "If you, O LORD, should mark iniquities, O Lord, who could stand?" The rhetorical answer is "no one" — Romans 3:23, "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." The gospel's answer is the imputed righteousness of Christ — 2 Corinthians 5:21 ("For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God"). We "stand" only because Christ stood condemned in our place.
4: A direct: "But with you there is forgiveness, that you may be feared." Forgiveness is Christ's gift alone. Acts 13:38 — "through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you." Colossians 1:14 — "in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins." The forgiveness that produces reverence (not presumption) is the forgiveness purchased at the cross — costly enough to be truly feared, free enough to be truly received.
5: A shadow: "I wait for the LORD, my soul waits, and in his word I hope." The intense waiting is Israel's messianic posture and now the church's eschatological posture. 1 Thessalonians 1:9-10 — the Thessalonians "turned to God... and to wait for his Son from heaven." Revelation 22:20 — "Come, Lord Jesus!" The "word" in which the hope resides is ultimately the Word made flesh (John 1:14) and the word of promise that he will return.
6: A shadow: "my soul waits for the Lord more than watchmen wait for the morning." The watchman straining for dawn is the image of eschatological yearning. Romans 8:23 — "we ourselves, who have the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for adoption as sons, the redemption of our bodies." The morning that ends the watch is the appearing of Christ (2 Peter 1:19 — "the morning star rises in your hearts").
7: A shadow: "For with the LORD there is steadfast love, and with him is plentiful redemption." Plentiful redemption — harbēh-ʿimmô p̄edût — is the sufficiency and overflow of the atonement. 1 Timothy 2:6 — Christ "gave himself as a ransom for all." Ephesians 1:7 — "In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins, in accordance with the riches of God's grace that he lavished on us." The plentiful redemption is the cross.
8: A direct: "And he will redeem Israel from all his iniquities." Matthew 1:21 — "you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins." The angel's announcement is the direct fulfillment of this verse. Titus 2:14 — Christ "gave himself for us to redeem us from all lawlessness." The God who promises in Psalms 130:8 to redeem Israel from all iniquity sends his Son in Matthew 1:21 to accomplish it. The verse and its fulfillment are inseparable.
